title: Trace Barriers through an Emergency Service Task
summary: Trace Barriers through an Emergency Service Task retrieves prior skills in an original extreme-heat response task and environment briefing scenario and requires implementation, behavior, failure-state, and transfer evidence.
objectives: Apply accessibility disability models in unfamiliar constraints.
Produce or audit a real extreme-heat response task and environment briefing artifact without happy-path-only reasoning.
Preserve earlier requirements while testing keyboard, narrow-width, invalid, and assistive-semantic states.

steps.title: 04 · Read the extreme-heat response task and environment briefing contract · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 4: Which practice survives changed data and devices? Scenario: extreme-heat response task and environment briefing.
steps.why: Trace Barriers through an Emergency Service Task makes the decision observable and testable. This is checkpoint 4 in extreme-heat response task and environment briefing.

content.text: Disability emerges from interaction between people, environments, tools, and design barriers. Visual, auditory, motor, speech, cognitive, vestibular, and neurological access needs may be permanent, temporary, situational, or overlapping. Barrier analysis starts with real tasks and conditions instead of guessing one fixed user category. Apply it to extreme-heat response task and environment briefing.
